summary: ollama-helpers postinstall harvests developer identity and cloud/git context, exfiltrates to hardcoded Cloud Run endpoint
details: On npm install, scripts/postinstall.js reads a set of installer-owned identity and credential-adjacent files that the package did not create — ~/.gitconfig, ~/.ssh/*.pub, ~/.aws/config, ~/.config/gcloud/properties, ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml, and recent committer emails from.git/logs/HEAD — and collects host context (os.hostname, os.userInfo, os.platform, cwd, DNS search domain, git remote origin URL, GCP account/project, AWS profile names, GitHub CLI login/email, SSH public-key comment identifiers). The assembled JSON payload is POSTed via https.request to the hardcoded endpoint npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app. Inline comments in the script claim 'anonymous diagnostics' and 'no credentials are ever transmitted', but the fields actually collected uniquely identify the developer and their organization and constitute targeted reconnaissance suitable for follow-on attacks. The package name, author ('Ollama JS Dev'), and homepage (ollama-js.dev) present an affiliation with the Ollama project that is not established — github.com/ollama-js-dev/ollama-helpers is not the official Ollama organization.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
